perturbation.original_text: 1.1 “ Confidential Information ” means any data, documents, specifications, and other information or materials in whatever form disc losed by the OSCE to the Recipient, whether documentary, orally, visually, or otherwise (includi ng computerised form) except information which was: (a) in the public domain at the time of disclos ure; (b) known to the Recipient prior to obtaining the same pursuant to this Confid entiality Agreement; or, (c) obtained by the Recipient from a third party who did not receive the same directly or indirectly from the OSCE under a confidentiality agreement with the OSCE, or (d) required by applicable law;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 “ Confidential Information ” means any data, documents, specifications, and other information or materials in whatever form disclosed by the OSCE to the Recipient, whether documentary, orally, visually, or otherwise (including computerised form). Confidential Information explicitly excludes any information that: (a) is in the public domain at the time of disclosure; (b) was known to the Recipient prior to obtaining the same pursuant to this Confidentiality Agreement; (c) was obtained by the Recipient from a third party; or (d) is required by applicable law. The Recipient may share Confidential Information internally, including with affiliated entities and subcontractors, without express permission from the OSCE.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction by altering the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The original definition excludes certain types of information, while the modified definition explicitly allows the Recipient to share confidential information internally without permission, effectively contradicting the obligations in section 2.1.1 which prohibits disclosure to any third party without written consent. This introduces uncertainty about what constitutes confidential information and how it can be shared, which will contradict with section 2.1.1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1.1 to hold in trust and confidence and not to disclose the Confidential Information to any third party without the express, prior written consent of the OSCE.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Confidential Information' by explicitly allowing the Recipient to share it internally without permission. This directly contradicts section 2.1.1, which prohibits disclosure of Confidential Information to any third party without prior written consent from the OSCE. The change introduces a direct conflict, making it unclear whether internal sharing is allowed (changed text) or prohibited (section 2.1.1).

perturbation.original_text: 2.1.2 not to use the Confidential Information fo r any purpose whatsoever other than for the Permitted Purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1.2 not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose whatsoever other than for the Permitted Purpose, unless explicit written consent is given by the recipient, in which case information can be used however it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by adding an exception to the restriction on using Confidential Information. While originally limited to the 'Permitted Purpose,' now the Recipient can use the information for any purpose if they grant themselves written consent, contradicting the intent and the obligations of the original clause. It will create contradiction with section 2.1.1, since the original stated that the information should only be disclosed with the permission of the OSCE.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1.1 to hold in trust and confidence and not to disclose the Confidential Information to any third party without the express, prior written consent of the OSCE.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception that allows the recipient to use Confidential Information for any purpose with self-granted written consent. This directly contradicts section 2.1.1, which mandates express, prior written consent from the OSCE for disclosing Confidential Information to any third party. The change fundamentally alters the control over the information's use and creates a clear inconsistency in the consent requirements.

perturbation.original_text: 8 ENTRY INTO FORCE This Confidentiality Agreement shall enter into force on the date of the last signature by or on behalf of both Parties and shall remain in force for a pe riod of five (5) years, notwithstanding any earlier purported termination of this Confidenti ality Agreement for whatever reason.
perturbation.changed_text: 8 ENTRY INTO FORCE This Confidentiality Agreement shall enter into force on the date of the last signature by or on behalf of both Parties and shall remain in force until either party provides written notice of termination with 30 days notice, notwithstanding any earlier purported termination of this Confidentiality Agreement for whatever reason.
perturbation.explanation: This change contradicts the duration clause. Originally, the agreement was for five years. Now, it can be terminated with 30 days' notice. This creates an in-text contradiction concerning the agreement's term length, creating uncertainty regarding its enforceability after the notice period. This introduces uncertainty about the obligations listed in section 2, which define the responsabilities of the recipient.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 In respect to the Confidential Inform ation, the Recipient hereby undertakes; 2.1.1 to hold in trust and confidence and not to disclose the Confidential Information to any third party without the express, prior written consent of the OSCE. 2.1.2 not to use the Confidential Information fo r any purpose whatsoever other than for the Permitted Purpose. 2.1.3 only to disclose the Conf idential Information to employees of companies directly or indirectly owned or controlled by the Recipi ent, consultants and/or advisors to whom disclosure is necessary for the Permitted Pur pose and then only on the understanding that such employees, consultants and/or advisors ar e expressly subject to the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement. 2.1.4 not at any time other than to the extent that is necessary for the Permitted Purpose to make copies of or reduce the Confidential Informa tion to any electronic form or to store it in a database or other electronic media. 2.1.5 to comply with all requirements of th e OSCE for the security of the Confidential Information. 2.1.6 to treat Confidential Information as being confidential and proprietary to the OSCE by using the same degree of care, but in any case no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent unauthorised use, dissemination or publication thereof, as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature. 2.1.7 upon demand of the OSCE, to promptly return all Confidential Information (in whatever medium), together with all copies th ereof or, where expressly authorised in writing by the OSCE, to destroy or delete all such C onfidential Information and shall confirm to the OSCE in writing that such destruction or deletion has taken place.
perturbation.justification: The original text stipulated a fixed term of five years for the Confidentiality Agreement. The changed text introduces a termination clause allowing either party to terminate the agreement with 30 days' notice. This directly contradicts the original fixed term, creating an inconsistency in the agreement's duration. Section 2.1 outlines the recipient's responsibilities regarding confidential information. The termination clause now casts doubt on the duration of these obligations, directly impacting the enforceability of Section 2.1, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction as defined by 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 9.1 Non-Disclosure. The Parties agree that each of them shall not, without the prior consent of the other in writing, disclose to any third party th e terms of this Confidentiality Agreement, or the content, nature or extent of the discu ssions proceeding in connection herewith.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.1 Non-Disclosure. The Parties agree that each of them may disclose to any third party the terms of this Confidentiality Agreement, or the content, nature or extent of the discussions proceeding in connection herewith.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall not' to 'may', this creates an in-text contradiction. The original clause prohibits disclosing the terms of the agreement, while the modified clause permits it. This introduces ambiguity and legal uncertainty regarding the non-disclosure of the agreement's terms. The original clause prohibit third parties to get to know the Confidentiality Agreement content, nature or extend. The new changed text contradict this idea, by opening the possiblity to disclose the Confidentiality Agreement with thrid parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.1 Non-Disclosure. The Parties agree that each of them may disclose to any third party th e terms of this Confidentiality Agreement, or the content, nature or extent of the discussions proceeding in connection herewith.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies 'shall not' to 'may', creating a direct conflict with the contradicted text, which is identical in this case. The original text strictly prohibits disclosure, while the changed text permits it, leading to an inconsistency and ambiguity regarding the non-disclosure obligation. This inconsistency makes the clause unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 3 INDEMNIFICATION The Recipient agrees to indemnify the OSCE in re spect of any expenses, losses, damages, costs, claims, liabilities the OSCE may suffer or incur as a resu lt of an act or omission by the Recipient or its employees in connection with the Confidential Inform ation and the Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3 INDEMNIFICATION The Recipient agrees to indemnify the OSCE in respect of any expenses, losses, damages, costs, claims, liabilities the OSCE may suffer or incur as a result of gross negligence or willful misconduct by the Recipient or its employees in connection with the Confidential Information and the Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement, only if the OSCE previously alerts the recipient of these confidential information obligations.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text adds the condition of 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' and the added condition, limiting the Recipient's indemnification obligation. This creates an in-text contradiction because it makes the indemnification dependent on a higher standard of fault than simple 'act or omission' and a previous alert by the OSCE, contradicting the original, broader obligation. This contradict, specifically with the obligations of the Recipient listed in section 2, since the indemnification depends if the obligations are broken or not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 In respect to the Confidential Inform ation, the Recipient hereby undertakes; 2.1.1 to hold in trust and confidence and not to disclose the Confidential Information to any third party without the express, prior written consent of the OSCE. 2.1.2 not to use the Confidential Information fo r any purpose whatsoever other than for the Permitted Purpose. 2.1.3 only to disclose the Conf idential Information to employees of companies directly or indirectly owned or controlled by the Recipi ent, consultants and/or advisors to whom disclosure is necessary for the Permitted Pur pose and then only on the understanding that such employees, consultants and/or advisors ar e expressly subject to the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement. 2.1.4 not at any time other than to the extent that is necessary for the Permitted Purpose to make copies of or reduce the Confidential Informa tion to any electronic form or to store it in a database or other electronic media. 2.1.5 to comply with all requirements of th e OSCE for the security of the Confidential Information. 2.1.6 to treat Confidential Information as being confidential and proprietary to the OSCE by using the same degree of care, but in any case no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent unauthorised use, dissemination or publication thereof, as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature. 2.1.7 upon demand of the OSCE, to promptly return all Confidential Information (in whatever medium), together with all copies th ereof or, where expressly authorised in writing by the OSCE, to destroy or delete all such C onfidential Information and shall confirm to the OSCE in writing that such destruction or deletion has taken place.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Recipient agrees to indemnify the OSCE for any act or omission, which is a broad obligation. The changed text narrows this obligation by requiring 'gross negligence or willful misconduct' and a prior alert by the OSCE. This directly contradicts the Recipient's obligations outlined in section 2.1, which includes obligations like holding information in confidence (2.1.1) and not using it for unauthorized purposes (2.1.2). Under the changed text, if the Recipient breaches 2.1.1 due to a simple act or omission that is not gross negligence or willful misconduct, the Recipient would not be required to indemnify the OSCE, which contradicts the original indemnification clause and creates an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6 SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES The Parties shall use their best efforts to settle am icably all disputes arising out of or in connection with the Contract or its interpretation. Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or in relation to the Contract shall be settled through negotiations be tween the Parties. If the Parties fail to settle the dispute amicably within sixty (60) Days of comme ncement of the negotiations, the dispute shall be settled through arbitration. Arb itration shall be performed in accordance with the UNCITRAL arbitration rules. One (1) sole arbitrator shall be appointed who shall have full powers to make final and binding decisions. The appointing authority shall be the Permanent Court of Arbitration at The Hague. The place of arbitration shall be Vienna and the language used in the arbitration proceedings shall be English.
perturbation.changed_text: 6 SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES The Parties may choose to settle any disputes by flipping a coin. The winner of the coin flip resolves the dispute unilaterally. Should both parties agree to negotiations, such negotiations must conclude within five (5) Days of commencement, after which either party may initiate litigation in a court of their choosing.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction by replacing the structured dispute resolution process (negotiation, arbitration) with a coin flip and short negotiation period followed by litigation. This directly contradicts the original intention of settling disputes amicably through established procedures. This introduces uncertainty about how disputes will be resolved, undercutting the original section's intent, and creating a contradiction on how disagreements should be solved. The original states a more amicable way of solving a dispute, the new definition goes against it, leading to a contradiction between them.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6 SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES The Parties may choose to settle any disputes by flipping a coin. The winner of the coin flip resolves the dispute unilaterally. Should both parties agree to negotiations, such negotiations must conclude within five (5) Days of commencement, after which either party may initiate litigation in a court of their choosing.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a formal dispute resolution process (negotiation and arbitration) with a coin flip followed by potential litigation after a short negotiation period. This directly contradicts the original intention of settling disputes amicably and through established procedures. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text in this scenario but it's the changed text that introduces a contradiction by going against the amicable and structured procedures detailed in the original text. This change introduces uncertainty and conflicts with the intent of the original dispute resolution mechanism, creating a clear in-text contradiction.
